content.text: Instruction tuning from the perspective of instruction-following behaviour asks a different question than the training mechanics do: what makes a model reliably follow diverse, novel instructions it has never seen? The answer, first systematically studied in FLAN (Finetuned Language Net, Wei et al., 2021), is that task diversity during tuning — not volume — is the primary driver of generalisation to new tasks. A model trained on 1 800 diverse instruction types zero-shot generalises to new task categories; a model trained on 1 task type 1 800 times does not.
The FLAN Finding: Diversity Drives Generalisation

FLAN converted 62 NLP datasets (classification, QA, translation, summarisation, NLI, commonsense) into a unified instruction format and fine-tuned a 137B model on all but a held-out cluster. The model then zero-shot solved tasks from the held-out cluster — tasks it had never trained on. The more task clusters included in tuning, the better the held-out zero-shot performance, plateauing around 20–30 task types.

A key practical challenge: the same task can be expressed in many ways. A model that only learned one template for sentiment analysis will fail when users phrase the request differently. Robust instruction tuning trains on multiple templates per task.

import anthropic
import random

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Multiple templates for the same underlying task
# (sentiment classification)
SENTIMENT_TEMPLATES = [
    "What is the sentiment of this review? {text}\nAnswer:",
    "Is the following text positive, negative, or neutral?\n\n{text}\n\nSentiment:",
    "Classify the emotional tone of this text as positive, negative, or neutral:\n{text}",
    "Review: {text}\n\nQuestion: How would you describe the sentiment?\nAnswer:",
    "Read the following and state whether it expresses a positive, negative, or neutral sentiment.\n\n{text}\n\nSentiment:",
]


def robust_sentiment_classify(text: str) -> str:
    """
    A well-instruction-tuned model should give the same answer
    regardless of which template is used — template robustness
    is a key property of good instruction following.
    """
    template = random.choice(SENTIMENT_TEMPLATES)
    prompt = template.format(text=text)

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=10,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text.strip().lower()


def test_template_robustness(text: str, n_trials: int = 5) -> dict:
    """Check how consistently the model answers across different phrasings."""
    results = [robust_sentiment_classify(text) for _ in range(n_trials)]
    unique = set(results)
    return {
        "results": results,
        "consistent": len(unique) == 1,
        "mode": max(set(results), key=results.count),
    }


print(test_template_robustness("The product is absolutely brilliant!"))
# {'results': ['positive', 'positive', 'positive', 'positive', 'positive'],
#  'consistent': True, 'mode': 'positive'}
62 NLP datasets reformatted as instructions. First large-scale demonstration that instruction tuning enables cross-task zero-shot generalisation.
1 600+ task types with explicit definitions, positive examples, and negative examples. Used to train Tk-INSTRUCT; showed that task definitions improve generalisation more than examples alone.
52 000 instruction-response pairs generated from GPT-3 text-davinci-003 using Self-Instruct. Showed that synthetically generated data is sufficient for basic instruction following.
1 800+ task types, combining prior datasets. Adds chain-of-thought templates to improve reasoning. Training mixture also includes dialog and program synthesis.
Community datasets of real user-model conversations, often distilled from GPT-4. Higher quality than Alpaca for open-domain dialogue.
What Good Instruction Following Looks Like
Instruction tuning reshapes behaviour but does not add facts not present in the base model. A model tuned on medical instructions still hallucinates medical facts it doesn't know.
A model that follows all instructions will also follow harmful ones. RLHF and constitutional AI are needed to instil the ability to refuse or redirect unsafe requests.
If the training responses in the dataset are biased toward agreeable, flattering answers, the model learns to be sycophantic rather than accurate. Dataset quality matters more than quantity.

The central insight from the FLAN line of research: instruction following is not a single skill but an abstraction over many task types. A model that has seen enough diverse tasks infers the pattern — "this is an instruction; I should execute it" — and generalises that pattern to new instructions it has never seen.
